Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Islamic Tradition

Ibn Arabi

1165 – 1240

Imagination, unveiling, and the soul’s ascent through divine reality.

Core thesis: Ibn ʿArabī’s philosophy of mind can be reconstructed as a theophanic, imaginal, and heart-centered theory of consciousness. Mind is not a private inner container opposed to the world. It is a graded mode of finding or awareness rooted in wujūd, the Real’s self-disclosure. Human cognition becomes truthful when the qalb—the heart as the locus of awareness—integrates reason, imagination, sense, revelation, and ethical transformation. Divine knowing and angelic intelligence are not optional “theological add-ons”; they are structurally central because, for Ibn ʿArabī, finite minds exist within a cosmic hierarchy of knowing whose source is God’s own knowledge, speech, light, and self-disclosure.

1. Methodological frame: not a system, but systematizable

Ibn ʿArabī did not write a single systematic treatise called “On Mind.” His works are vast, layered, allusive, and often deliberately polyvalent. The Fuṣūṣ al-ḥikam became the standard compact text for transmitting his teaching, with twenty-seven prophetic chapters and a large commentary tradition; the al-Futūḥāt al-Makkiyya is described by the Muhyiddin Ibn Arabi Society as a vast compendium of metaphysics, cosmology, spiritual anthropology, psychology, jurisprudence, ritual meaning, divine names, and cosmic hierarchy. The same source notes that, although about eighty-five extant works can be treated as securely genuine, lists attributed to Ibn ʿArabī point to roughly three hundred works, with many textual complications over the centuries.

This matters because a “philosophy of mind” must be extracted by disciplined reconstruction. Ibn ʿArabī’s own writing resists reduction to a single closed doctrine; MIAS summarizes this well, noting that his ideas “do not allow themselves to be reduced to a system,” while also listing recurring themes such as divine names, existence and nonexistence, transcendence and immanence, modes of knowing, human perfection, and barzakh, the in-between.

The best guiding category is taḥqīq, realization. In the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy account by William Chittick, taḥqīq is the “school of realization,” derived from ḥaqq and ḥaqīqa: truth, reality, rightness, appropriateness. It is not merely propositional accuracy. It means knowing the truth of cosmos, soul, and human affairs in light of the Supreme Reality, and acting in accordance with the proper ḥaqq of each thing. In this sense, Ibn ʿArabī’s theory of mind is inseparable from ontology and ethics: to know rightly is to become rightly configured.

2. The central ontological move: wujūd as being, finding, and awareness

The decisive starting point is wujūd. In philosophical Arabic, wujūd comes to mean existence or being, but Chittick emphasizes that in Qurʾānic and ordinary Arabic it also means finding, becoming conscious of, encountering, enjoying, and being ecstatic. For Ibn ʿArabī, “finding”—perception, awareness, consciousness—is never absent from “being found.” This does not mean that every finite thing is a little Cartesian subject. It means that existence and manifestation are inseparable from divine light and finding.

This gives us the first axiom of an Akbarian philosophy of mind: consciousness is not a late emergent property added to brute matter. Rather, finite cognition is a derivative, delimited participation in a more primordial noetic reality. Ibn ʿArabī’s language of Light is crucial here. The SEP passage reports Ibn ʿArabī’s view that smell, taste, imagination, memory, reason, reflection, conceptualization, and every faculty through which perception occurs are “light”; and that, since the Real is Light, every known thing has a relation with the Real.

This is not simple pantheism. Ibn ʿArabī’s tawḥīd affirms that there is no real wujūd but God, but the same framework insists on the difference between the nondelimited Real and delimited existents. The Real is incomparable and transcendent, while also disclosing itself in all things. Chittick also cautions that the later slogan waḥdat al-wujūd should not be treated as a simple label that Ibn ʿArabī himself used as a technical doctrine; the term became controversial through later debates and polemics.

A helpful philosophical formulation is this: Ibn ʿArabī is not saying that God, world, and minds are all the same thing in a flat identity. He is saying that finite beings are real as self-disclosures, images, loci, relations, and delimitations of the one Real. A finite mind is therefore neither self-sufficient substance nor mere illusion; it is an imaginal, relational, and ethically transformable locus of divine disclosure.

3. The “three books”: scripture, cosmos, soul

Ibn ʿArabī’s philosophy of mind is also a hermeneutics. God’s speech is not confined to written scripture. Chittick’s SEP account states that divine speech reveals itself in scripture, the universe, and the soul; Qurʾānic “signs” or āyāt designate phenomena of the universe, inner events of the soul, and verses of revelation. God “voices three books,” each composed of signs.

This means the mind is not simply a representational machine. It is a reader of signs. Human cognition is interpretive because reality itself is semiotic: cosmos, soul, and scripture are mutually illuminating books. To understand the world is to read divine speech; to understand oneself is also to read divine speech; to read scripture properly is to gain the key for interpreting both world and self.

The same SEP passage reports Ibn ʿArabī’s view that meanings in the “three books” are never repeated, and that creation is renewed “at every breath.” This gives his philosophy of mind a dynamic temporality: cognition is not merely the recognition of fixed objects, but participation in ever-renewed self-disclosures.

4. Vocabulary of soul and mind

There is no single Akbarian equivalent of the modern word “mind.” Several terms carry different parts of the field.

Nafs means soul, self, psyche, and sometimes the egoic or passionate self. In Ibn ʿArabī’s larger architecture, the human nafs is imaginal: the meeting point of spirit and body. The SEP account states that the human book’s world of imagination is identical with the soul/self, because human experience is simultaneously spiritual and bodily. The soul can move upward toward spirit or downward toward matter because it is an in-between reality.

Rūḥ, spirit, is the higher luminous principle, but it is not simply an abstract intellect detached from embodiment. Souad Hakim’s MIAS essay describes the human spirit as open to light and darkness, managing the human being with all material and spiritual components. In that account, polishing the heart and freeing the soul from passion allow the spirit to receive divine knowledge and connect to the supra-sensible world.

Qalb, heart, is the central organ of consciousness. It is not merely emotion, nor merely a physical organ, nor merely “subjectivity.” It is the locus of awareness, receptivity, fluctuation, and divine self-disclosure. In Chittick’s SEP account, the heart has two eyes: reason and imagination. Either eye alone distorts perception; the heart must see God’s incomparability with reason and God’s similarity or immanence with imagination.

ʿAql means intellect or intelligence, but it should not be reduced to modern rationalism. Chittick warns that translating ʿaql simply as “reason” risks assimilating Islamic intellect to Cartesian raison. In the Islamic philosophical and Akbarian context, intelligence has an ontological dimension: recognition is not merely an operation of the mind but the existence of the mind and heart insofar as intelligence is a radiance of God.

At the same time, ʿaql in its delimiting function can become a “fetter.” Ibn ʿArabī’s Arabic text in the Fuṣūṣ, in the chapter on Shuʿayb, says that the Qurʾānic reminder is for one “who has a heart,” not one who merely has intellect, because intellect binds or confines the matter to one determination, while reality refuses confinement.

Khayāl, imagination or image, is perhaps the most important term for a philosophy of mind. It does not mean fantasy. It means image, imaginal faculty, dream-form, mirror-form, intermediate world, and, in the widest sense, the ontological condition of everything other than God. Chittick stresses that Ibn ʿArabī criticizes philosophers and theologians for failing to acknowledge imagination’s cognitive and ontological significance.

Wahm, often translated estimation or supposition, should also not be ignored. An Arabic study in Ḥikma argues that Ibn ʿArabī adopts a middle position toward wahm: he neither simply rejects it nor uncritically accepts it. Rather, he treats it as a cognitive power with defined limits, capable of contributing to knowledge but also of producing error when it exceeds its proper scope. That article is useful because much Anglophone reception emphasizes imagination and heart, while Arabic discussions sometimes linger more carefully over wahm, faculty-boundaries, and the critique of cognitive overreach.

Dhikr, remembrance or invocation, is not just verbal repetition. In the Fuṣūṣ chapter on Jonah, Ibn ʿArabī says that true remembrance flows through the whole servant, not only the tongue; if only the tongue remembers, God is “companion” to the tongue in that respect, while other parts may remain heedless. This is an important claim for embodied and distributed cognition: attention, remembrance, and presence can be partial or integrated across the whole person.

5. The heart as the center of consciousness

The heart is the decisive human center because it is capable of fluctuation. This is not instability in the negative sense. It is cognitive flexibility adequate to a reality that discloses itself in ever-new forms. Ibn ʿArabī’s Arabic Fuṣūṣ text says the heart of the knower can “contain” the Real in the sense that, when the Real is witnessed in self-disclosure, the heart cannot attend to anything else; he also links the heart’s breadth to the diversification of divine self-disclosure.

James Morris’s MIAS essay on prayer and the heart frames the problem in experiential terms. For Ibn ʿArabī, all experience is ultimately theophany from the widest metaphysical point of view, but most people do not ordinarily experience it that way. The question is how the heart transforms everyday perception into contemplation and theophany. Morris emphasizes that this transformation is a divine opening or illumination, not merely a psychological technique.

Jane Clark’s MIAS essay similarly stresses that, in the Sufi context, the heart is a receptive “place”—not simply a physical location—where God reveals Himself to the human being. This heart-knowledge is dhawq and kashf, taste and unveiling, and it is superior to merely discursive reason without excluding intellect. Ibn ʿArabī’s famous “Yes—No” response to Ibn Rushd captures precisely that relation: reason is not abolished, but it cannot exhaust the highest knowledge.

So the heart is the Akbarian answer to the “unity of consciousness” problem. It unifies without homogenizing. It allows reason to distinguish and imagination to unite, law to differentiate and unveiling to disclose, sensory forms to be interpreted and spiritual meanings to be embodied. A malformed mind absolutizes one faculty. A realized heart gives each faculty its ḥaqq.

6. Reason: necessary, luminous, and limited

Ibn ʿArabī is not anti-intellectual. His writing is too conceptually intricate for that caricature to survive. But he is strongly opposed to the sovereignty of discursive reason when it claims to define the Real. The problem is not intellect as such; it is delimiting reason detached from heart, imagination, revelation, and realization.

Chittick’s MIAS essay makes the crucial distinction: ʿaql has an ontological dimension and is not equivalent to modern rationalism. Intelligence “finding” the reality of things within itself is both awareness and the very existence of mind and heart; in that respect intelligence is the radiance of God. Ibn ʿArabī recognizes the philosophers’ pursuit, but sees the human reality as more extensive than their ideal of union with the First Intellect.

The SEP account sharpens this point: Ibn ʿArabī accepts the general philosophical picture of the rational soul’s unlimited potential, but considers the Avicennian ideal of becoming an intellective world “barren” because it neglects the intermediary and sensible realms, which are essentially imaginal rather than merely intelligible. He says imagination is the widest known thing because it gives form even to impossibility, necessity, possibility, existence, and nonexistence.

In a reconstructed Akbarian philosophy of mind, reason has at least four proper roles: discernment of difference, protection against crude anthropomorphism, legal-ethical discrimination, and conceptual clarification. Its failure mode is fixation: it mistakes its own determinate formulation for the Real. The mature mind is not irrational; it is trans-rational in the strict sense that reason is retained but integrated into a wider heart-intelligence.

7. Imagination as cognitive faculty and ontological realm

The single most important difference between Ibn ʿArabī and many modern accounts of mind is that imagination is not subjective fantasy. It is an organ of truth when disciplined, and it corresponds to an actual level of reality.

Chittick explains that khayāl can mean mental faculty, mirror image, shadow, dream image, or ontological image. An image unites two sides: it is both the mirror and the reflected object, or neither simply mirror nor object. A dream is both the soul and what is seen, or neither simply the soul nor what is seen. Imagination can therefore perceive what reason’s binary true/false logic cannot: things that are simultaneously “He/not He,” real/unreal, face/veil.

Ibn ʿArabī’s Fuṣūṣ chapter on Joseph provides the primary architecture. It says the luminous wisdom of Joseph spreads over the presence of imagination, which is among the first beginnings of divine revelation. True dreams are not dismissed as irrational debris; they are an imaginal form of disclosure requiring interpretation. The famous theme “people are asleep, and when they die they awaken” is deployed to present worldly experience itself as dream-like and interpretive.

The same Arabic passage treats angelic appearance through imagination. When the angel appears as a man, he is not simply “really a man” nor simply “not a man.” He is an angel entering a human image; the knower interprets the image to its truth. Ibn ʿArabī says both descriptions—“this is a man” and “this is Gabriel”—are true under different respects.

This is central for philosophy of mind. Imagination is a transductive faculty: it lets spiritual realities become sensibly formed and sensible realities become spiritually interpretable. It explains dream, vision, prophecy, angelic encounter, symbolic cognition, afterlife embodiment, and ordinary perception as participation in a world of images. In modern terms, imagination is neither mere mental imagery nor fictional construction; it is the barzakhic medium through which realities cross ontological registers.

8. Barzakh: the in-between as the key to mind-body problems

Barzakh means isthmus, interval, limit, or in-between. It divides and unites without being reducible to either side. For Ibn ʿArabī, imagination is the supreme barzakh. The cosmos itself is an in-between between the infinite light of Existence and the darkness of nonexistence. The human soul is likewise in-between spirit and body, light and darkness, wakefulness and sleep, knowledge and ignorance.

This gives Ibn ʿArabī a sophisticated alternative to Cartesian dualism. The SEP account explicitly notes that Ibn ʿArabī’s foregrounding of the in-between realm helped prevent Islamic philosophy from falling into a strict mind/body dichotomy. The imaginal world is a real external realm where spiritual beings can be corporealized, as in angelic appearance, and corporeal beings can be spiritualized, as in posthumous pleasure or pain.

The mind-body problem is therefore reframed. The hard boundary is not between immaterial mind and material body. The real structure is a hierarchy of presences: spiritual, imaginal, and visible. Human mind is not disembodied intellect; it is embodied, soulish, imaginal, and spiritual at once. The “bridge” is not an added mechanism between two alien substances; the soul itself is bridge-like.

9. The divine “mind”: careful reconstruction

It is tempting to say Ibn ʿArabī believes in the “mind of God.” That phrase is usable only with care. In Islamic theological language, God is not a finite subject with a mental apparatus. God is not “a mind” among minds. The better Akbarian language is divine knowledge, divine speech, divine command, divine names, Light, self-disclosure, and the Real as the source of all finding.

The divine Essence is not knowable in its quiddity. The SEP account states that humans can know the divine names but not the Essence named by the names; of the Essence one can know “that it is,” not “what it is.” The names indicate both transcendence and immanence: in one respect they mark incomparability; in another they denote attributes such as mercy, knowledge, life, forgiveness, and vengeance.

God’s knowing is not an inner representational process unfolding in time. Ibn ʿArabī holds that God knows universals and particulars eternally; even the falling leaf is known always and forever. The fixed entities, aʿyān thābita, are things inasmuch as they are nonexistent in themselves but known to God; when the engendering command “Be!” occurs, the same entity becomes manifest as delimited existence.

The “mind of God,” then, can be translated philosophically as the absolute noetic ground: God’s self-knowledge, knowledge of all possible and actual determinations, speech as creative command, and Light as the condition of all perception. This is not a psychological deity thinking discursively. It is divine knowing as the ontological source of all knowability and all finite cognition.

A further nuance: in the later Akbarian tradition, the Reality of Realities or Muhammadan Reality is treated as the comprehensive mediating reality. Chittick’s SEP account explains that in God this Reality is divine knowledge of all things, while in the cosmos it is the ever-changing totality of temporal creation; followers spoke of God’s self-disclosure to Himself in Himself as the self-knowledge by which He knows the concomitants of His infinity.

10. Angelic and cosmic minds

Angelic cognition is not a marginal supernatural ornament in Ibn ʿArabī. It belongs to the structure of noetic reality. Angels, the First Intellect, the Pen, the Universal Soul, and the Guarded Tablet are ways of articulating cosmic intelligence, transmission, inscription, and imaginal manifestation.

Arabic Akbarian lexicographical material identifies the First Intellect with the Highest Pen and describes it as the first cosmic teacher: it receives from God and writes what it knows in the Guarded Tablet. The Tablet, called the Universal Soul, is the first being that learns from a created teacher and is described as the first receptive existent proceeding from the Intellect.

A Hindawi Arabic discussion presents the Guarded Tablet or Universal Soul as the first manifestation of the First Intellect and second level of manifestation in the ʿamāʾ. The First Intellect is described as the universal spirit from which partial spirits branch, while the Universal Soul is the source from which particular souls governing sensible bodies branch. It also distinguishes the Universal Soul’s scientific and practical powers, reinforcing its role as a cosmic cognitive mediator.

This hierarchy should not be flattened into modern “entities with brains.” The First Intellect is cosmic noesis; the Universal Soul is cosmic receptivity, inscription, and governance; angels are noetic-spiritual agents and mediators of divine command. Their cognition is part of how God’s speech becomes legible in worlds.

The human being, however, is not simply inferior to the angelic intellect. Arabic Akbarian material reports Ibn ʿArabī’s claim that when God created the Perfect Human, He gave him the rank of the First Intellect and taught him what the Intellect did not know concerning the formal reality connected to the Real; the First Intellect, despite its greatness, is only a part of the total human image.

This is a major philosophical point. Pure intellect is not the highest possible mind because it is not all-comprehensive. The Perfect Human surpasses the merely intellectual by integrating intelligible, imaginal, bodily, ethical, revelatory, and divine-name dimensions. Human perfection is not angelic abstraction; it is comprehensive barzakhic integration.

11. Prophetic mind and revelation

For Ibn ʿArabī, prophecy is not reducible to moral genius or social legislation. It is a supreme form of cognitive receptivity, where divine speech, angelic mediation, imaginal embodiment, and human heart converge. This has direct philosophy-of-mind relevance because it treats revelation as a mode of knowledge rather than as a noncognitive interruption.

Souad Hakim’s MIAS essay lists many forms of inspired cognitive emanation in Ibn ʿArabī: divine discourse, revelation without new law, conversation, divine dictation, sincere inspiration, dream-vision, a breath blown into the heart, unveiling, witnessing, visible sign, night journey, and ascension. It also notes that such knowledge may be received through different sensory modalities, including hearing, sight, and smell.

This gives an Akbarian account of altered and intensified cognition without reducing it to hallucination. The key is discernment: images must be interpreted, faculties must remain within their limits, and revelation must be integrated with prophetic guidance. The prophetic mind is not the negation of imagination; it is imagination disciplined by divine command and heart-realization.

Ibn ʿArabī’s own Fuṣūṣ introduction also matters methodologically. He presents the Fuṣūṣ as received through spiritual casting and blowing, while not claiming legislative prophecy. That posture shapes the epistemic status of his writings: they are offered as inspired unveiling within the bounds of Muhammadan inheritance, not as a new scripture or new law.

12. Human perfection: the Perfect Human as all-comprehensive mind

The Perfect Human, al-insān al-kāmil, is the culmination of Ibn ʿArabī’s philosophy of mind. The Perfect Human is not merely a saintly personality. He is the all-comprehensive barzakh between the Real and the cosmos. Chittick’s SEP account quotes Ibn ʿArabī: man has one relation entering the divine level and one entering the cosmic level; he is a barzakh between cosmos and Real, bringing together creation and the Real.

The same account describes the Perfect Human as the spirit animating the cosmos: the whole cosmos is the differentiation of Adam, and Adam is the all-comprehensive book; in relation to the cosmos, the human is like spirit in relation to body.

Later Akbarian schematizations of the Five Divine Presences make this explicit. The unseen, imaginal, and visible worlds correspond in the human being to spirit, soul, and body, while the fixed entity or reality of the human corresponds to the Reality of Realities. In other words, the human being is a microcosmic synthesis of the levels of existence.

This yields a strong anthropological claim: the fullest mind is not the most abstract mind, but the most comprehensive one. It can know by reason, imagination, sense, unveiling, prophetic inheritance, ethical action, and divine-name realization. The perfected mind is a cosmos in miniature because the human being is the place where all presences can be gathered, balanced, and consciously realized.

13. Self-knowledge and the reality of the soul

The famous formula “whoever knows himself knows his Lord” is not a decorative maxim for Ibn ʿArabī. In the Fuṣūṣ chapter on Shuʿayb, he links true self-knowledge to knowledge of the Lord and says that the reality of the soul has not been discovered by speculative thinkers; discursive reflection alone does not yield the essence of the soul.

This is not anti-philosophy. It is a claim about the kind of access required. The soul is not fully known as an object among objects because the soul’s reality is bound up with divine image, self-disclosure, and realization. To know the soul truly is to undergo transformation in which the knower, known, and mode of knowing are reconfigured.

Selfhood is also dynamic. The same Arabic passage discusses the world’s renewal “with the breaths,” the new creation that most people fail to perceive. This supports an Akbarian process account of identity: the self persists, but not as a static substance immune to transformation. It is renewed in forms while grounded in a deeper reality.

A Persian comparison of Ibn ʿArabī and Mullā Ṣadrā reinforces this dynamic anthropology. It describes the soul as having two directions, one toward the higher world and one toward the lower; the body is related to spirit because it is the locus of the divine breathing-in of spirit and intellect; the soul is above body and below intellect, a field in which thoughts, desires, sciences, and actions grow from seeds cast through spirit.

14. Embodiment, death, and posthumous cognition

Ibn ʿArabī’s anthropology is not a simple escape from body. The Fuṣūṣ chapter on Jonah says that the complete human formation—spirit, body, and soul—was created by God according to His form. It also emphasizes preserving the human formation and treating it with compassion.

The same chapter says that, while the human being remains alive, perfection can still be acquired. Death is not annihilation but separation and transition; God gives the person another vehicle suited to the abode into which he moves.

This has major philosophy-of-mind implications. The body is not merely a disposable shell, nor is cognition reducible to the present physical body. Rather, embodiment occurs across levels: elemental body, subtle spirit, imaginal body, and posthumous vehicle. The human mind is always embodied in some mode, but “body” itself is not restricted to gross physical matter.

15. Ethics, affect, and transformation of mind

In Ibn ʿArabī, ethics is cognitive. Character is not an external addition to knowledge; it determines what the soul can receive and how it interprets self-disclosure. Chittick’s MIAS essay on compassion explains that the path is “becoming characterized by the character traits of God,” and that tazkiyat al-nafs, purification of the soul, means removing ugly character traits and actualizing divine traits.

This connects moral psychology to ontology. The divine names are not abstractions; they are the roots of qualities such as mercy, beauty, knowledge, power, justice, and compassion. A person’s mind is formed by which names and traits become actual in the soul. Vice constricts cognition; virtue expands it. The more the soul becomes characterized by divine traits, the more it can receive the Real appropriately.

Freedom and responsibility also belong here. Chittick’s SEP account states that the Real’s prescriptive commands introduce causal factors that make human beings responsible for what they become morally and spiritually; humans are “compelled to be free,” and their conformity to the command determines their levels in the return.

Ibn ʿArabī even treats focused spiritual intention, himma, with caution. In the Fuṣūṣ chapter on Lot, he says that higher knowledge reduces manipulative deployment of himma: the more knowledge increases, the less the knower tries to impose willful psychical control, because he realizes servanthood and the unity of actor and acted-upon.

16. The imaginal and poetic mind

Ibn ʿArabī’s theory of mind also includes symbolic and poetic cognition. This is not ornamental. Claude Addas’s French MIAS essay argues that poetry occupies a major place in Ibn ʿArabī’s work and functions as a doctrinal support. She links poetic language to the imaginal world, where spiritual realities take form, and says that poetry is a privileged means of “traveling” in the imaginal world and conveying supra-formal realities.

Addas also stresses that the imaginal world is not “imagination” in the ordinary sense of combining sensory images. In the “Earth of Reality,” bodies are subtle and intelligibles take on form; its fantastic descriptions should not deceive us into treating it as mythic unreality, because it is as real in its order as the ground beneath our feet is in ours.

This is an important supplement to Anglophone discussions of “creative imagination.” The poetic mind is not merely expressive; it is epistemic. It can carry what discursive prose cannot fully stabilize: polyvalent, ambivalent, symbolic, and imaginal truths whose nature exceeds univocal conceptual representation.

17. Non-English reception: useful emphases

The Anglophone reception, especially through Chittick, Morris, and Corbin, tends to highlight wujūd, realization, heart, and imagination. That is indispensable. But Arabic, Persian, and French materials bring out some additional emphases.

Arabic discussions often pay closer attention to the internal taxonomy of cognitive powers. The Ḥikma article on the limits of reason and the authority of wahm argues that Ibn ʿArabī examines each cognitive faculty, sets boundaries for it, distinguishes mediated reason from a receptive “self” intellect, and shows a relative preference for imagination and heart without abolishing the other powers.

Arabic Hindawi material on absolute imagination emphasizes the intermediary status of levels such as ʿamāʾ, fixed entities, and absolute imagination. It argues that these intellectual or imaginal levels are not less important than sensible existence and that they block a simplistic modern reading of Ibn ʿArabī as a crude pantheist.

Persian material, especially in comparison with Mullā Ṣadrā, tends to underline mediation between soul and body: vaporous spirit, imaginal barzakh, body as vehicle or field, and the soul’s dual orientation upward and downward. This is valuable for reconstructing embodiment and psychosomatic continuity.

A Persian article on the imaginal world states that Ibn ʿArabī, after Suhrawardī, developed the imaginal realm in a way that influenced later transcendent philosophy; it describes imagination as joining opposites—intellect and material sensation—and places prophetic visions, angelic encounters, inspiration, revelation, and ascension in the detached imaginal world.

French discussions, especially in the orbit of Corbin and Addas, often linger on the mirror, poetry, and imaginal form. A French philosophical article on the polishing of the mirror of the soul treats the mirror not as a harmless metaphor, but as a way of understanding the soul as a theater of divine representation whose capacity must be polished to receive what exceeds it.

18. Extracted Akbarian philosophy of mind: twelve theses

First, being and awareness are co-implicated. Wujūd is not brute presence. It is finding, manifestation, and divine light. Finite consciousness is derivative of the Real’s self-disclosing light, not an isolated mental substance.

Second, God is not “a mind,” but the absolute noetic source. The divine “mind,” if the phrase is used, means God’s eternal knowledge, speech, command, self-disclosure, and Light. God’s Essence is unknowable in quiddity; divine names are knowable as modes of disclosure.

Third, cosmic and angelic cognition are structurally real. The First Intellect, Pen, Universal Soul, Guarded Tablet, angels, and prophetic revelation articulate a hierarchy of noetic mediation. These are not detachable mythic decorations; they are part of the Akbarian account of how knowledge, form, command, and manifestation traverse worlds.

Fourth, the human mind is microcosmic. The human being gathers spirit, soul, body, visible, imaginal, and unseen levels. The perfected mind is not merely rational; it is all-comprehensive.

Fifth, the heart is the center of consciousness. The heart integrates reason and imagination, transcendence and immanence, law and unveiling, sensory perception and divine signification. The heart’s fluctuation is not cognitive weakness but a necessary adaptation to ever-renewed self-disclosure.

Sixth, reason is luminous but limited. ʿAql as intelligence is divine radiance; ʿaql as delimiting reason becomes a fetter if it claims finality. Reason must distinguish, but it must not imprison reality in one conceptual form.

Seventh, imagination is ontological. Khayāl is not fantasy. It is image, faculty, world, and cosmic condition. It explains dreams, visions, angelic forms, symbols, resurrection, bodily-spiritual continuity, and the world’s “He/not He” character.

Eighth, mind is barzakhic. The soul is an in-between reality, neither reducible to body nor separable from embodiment. This dissolves strict mind/body dualism by inserting imaginal mediation.

Ninth, knowledge is realization. True knowing is not merely representing propositions. It is actualizing the soul’s potential, giving each thing its proper ḥaqq, and becoming appropriately configured to the Real.

Tenth, ethics is cognitive formation. Character traits shape what and how the soul can know. Purification of the soul and becoming characterized by divine traits are not moral addenda; they are conditions of clear perception.

Eleventh, selfhood is dynamic renewal. The soul persists through transformations, but the world and self are renewed at every breath. Identity is continuity through imaginal and divine-name configurations, not static substance in the modern sense.

Twelfth, prophecy is a supreme mode of cognition. Revelation, angelic mediation, true dreams, inspiration, unveiling, and ascension belong to the high end of the same noetic cosmos. They must be discerned and interpreted, not dismissed as noncognitive.

19. Relation to contemporary philosophy of mind

Ibn ʿArabī is not a physicalist. The physical is one level of manifestation, not the ontological base from which consciousness somehow emerges.

He is not a Cartesian dualist. Mind and body are not two alien substances needing an external bridge; the soul is already imaginal and barzakhic, and embodiment itself has multiple levels.

He is not a standard idealist either, because “mental” and “material” are not his basic categories. The more basic categories are Real/disclosure, unseen/visible, image/barzakh, name/entity, heart/faculty, and realization/veiling.

He is not simply a panpsychist. A loose comparison may be tempting because awareness is ontologically basic, but Ibn ʿArabī’s doctrine is more theocentric and hierarchical. There is no true finder but God; creatures participate in finding according to preparedness.

He is closer to a theophanic noetic realism: all finite cognition is a mode of participation in divine light; all objects are signs and images; all minds are situated within a hierarchy of divine, angelic, imaginal, embodied, and ethical disclosure.

20. Most promising research program

A rigorous contemporary extraction should proceed in five linked studies.

First, map the faculties: qalb, ʿaql, khayāl, wahm, fikr, ḥiss, dhikr, kashf, dhawq, himma, nafs, and rūḥ. This should include Arabic discussions of faculty-limits, not only Anglophone discussions of imagination.

Second, build an ontology of cognition from wujūd, Light, divine speech, the three books, fixed entities, and the divine names.

Third, build a layered model of mind: divine noesis, First Intellect/Pen, Universal Soul/Tablet, angelic cognition, prophetic cognition, saintly unveiling, ordinary human cognition, and posthumous cognition.

Fourth, build an Akbarian account of embodied consciousness using barzakh, imaginal body, subtle spirit, death as transition, and remembrance distributed through the whole person.

Fifth, compare this model with contemporary debates: panpsychism, neutral monism, enactivism, embodied cognition, predictive imagination, phenomenology, virtue epistemology, and philosophy of religion. The comparison should be cautious: Ibn ʿArabī’s theory is not a medieval anticipation of any one modern school, but it can challenge modern assumptions about mind by refusing to separate consciousness, ontology, ethics, symbol, and revelation.

The resulting philosophy of mind would be neither secularized Sufism nor uncritical theology. It would be an Akbarian noetics: a theory of how finite awareness, divine knowledge, imaginal mediation, angelic intelligence, embodied soul, and ethical realization belong to one graded order of disclosure.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
